title: JWT
description: JSON Web Token — a compact, URL-safe token format (header.payload.signature) used to transmit claims between parties, commonly for API authentication and s

title: JWS
description: JSON Web Signature — the signed JWT format defined in RFC 7515. Most JWTs in production are JWS tokens with a cryptographic signature.

title: JWE
description: JSON Web Encryption — an encrypted token format (RFC 7516) where the payload is encrypted, not just signed. Used when token contents must remain confidenti

title: JWK
description: JSON Web Key — a JSON data structure representing a cryptographic key. Used in JWKS documents for RS256/ES256 verification.

title: JWKS
description: JSON Web Key Set — a JSON document containing an array of public keys (JWKs). OIDC providers publish JWKS at /.well-known/jwks.json for token verification.

title: Claim
description: A name/value pair inside the JWT payload. Registered claims (exp, sub, iss) are standardized; private claims are custom to your application.

title: Header
description: The first segment of a JWT, Base64URL-encoded JSON typically containing alg (algorithm) and typ (token type). Always verify alg before trusting a token.

title: Payload
description: The second segment of a JWT containing claims — user ID, roles, expiration, issuer, and custom data. Readable by anyone; never store secrets here.

title: Signature
description: The third segment of a JWT, created by signing header.payload with a secret (HS256) or private key (RS256). Proves integrity and authenticity.

title: alg
description: Algorithm header claim specifying how the signature was created (e.g. HS256, RS256, ES256). Must match your verification method.

title: exp
description: Expiration Time claim — Unix timestamp after which the token must be rejected. Always validate server-side.

title: iss
description: Issuer claim — identifies who created the token, usually your auth server URL. Validate against expected issuer.

title: aud
description: Audience claim — identifies intended recipients (API identifier or client ID). Prevents token misuse across services.

title: sub
description: Subject claim — unique identifier for the user or entity the token represents.

title: iat
description: Issued At claim — Unix timestamp when the token was created. Useful for freshness checks and debugging.

title: nbf
description: Not Before claim — Unix timestamp before which the token must not be accepted. Prevents premature use.

title: jti
description: JWT ID claim — unique token identifier for revocation lists and replay prevention.

title: kid
description: Key ID header parameter — references which key in a JWKS was used to sign the token.

title: HS256
description: HMAC-SHA256 symmetric signing algorithm. Same secret signs and verifies. Simple but requires secure secret distribution.

title: RS256
description: RSA-SHA256 asymmetric signing. Private key signs; public key verifies. Standard for OIDC and enterprise auth.

title: ES256
description: ECDSA with P-256 and SHA-256. Compact keys, widely used by Apple, Google, and modern OIDC providers.

title: Base64URL
description: URL-safe Base64 encoding used in JWTs. Replaces +/ with -_ and omits padding. Not encryption — anyone can decode.

title: Bearer Token
description: HTTP Authorization scheme where the client sends Authorization: Bearer <token>. Whoever holds the token gains access.

title: Access Token
description: Short-lived token authorizing API access. OAuth 2.0 access tokens are often JWTs but can be opaque strings.

title: Refresh Token
description: Long-lived token used to obtain new access tokens without re-authentication. Must be stored securely and rotatable.

title: ID Token
description: OpenID Connect token containing user identity claims. Always a JWT. Not used for API authorization — use access tokens instead.

title: OpenID Connect (OIDC)
description: Identity layer on OAuth 2.0. Adds ID tokens, standardized claims, and discovery endpoints including JWKS.

title: OAuth 2.0
description: Authorization framework for delegated access. Often issues JWT access tokens but does not require JWT format.

title: Scope
description: OAuth permission string (e.g. openid profile email) defining what access a token grants.

title: Token Endpoint
description: OAuth endpoint (/oauth/token) where clients exchange credentials or codes for access and refresh tokens.

title: Clock Skew
description: Small time difference between servers. JWT libraries typically allow 30–60 seconds leeway on exp/nbf validation.

title: none Algorithm Attack
description: Security vulnerability where attackers set alg:none to bypass signature verification. Always reject unsigned tokens.

title: Key Rotation
description: Periodic replacement of signing keys. JWKS may contain multiple keys; kid header identifies the active one.

title: Token Revocation
description: Invalidating tokens before expiry via blocklists, short TTLs, or refresh token rotation. JWTs are stateless — revocation requires extra design.

title: PKCE
description: Proof Key for Code Exchange — OAuth extension preventing authorization code interception. Required for public clients (SPAs, mobile).

title: .well-known
description: Standard path for OIDC discovery (/.well-known/openid-configuration) and JWKS (/.well-known/jwks.json) endpoints.

title: RFC 7519
description: The IETF standard defining JSON Web Token (JWT) structure and registered claims.

title: RFC 7515
description: The IETF standard for JSON Web Signature (JWS) — how JWT signatures are created and verified.
